turns.content: I have a question... about something my supplier told me. he says there might be new regulations on cosmetic products for barbershops. like... we need to show certificates for the products we use. do you know anything about this?

There have been ongoing changes to cosmetics regulations in Turkey — the Turkish Medicines and Medical Devices Agency (TİTCK) has been tightening oversight of cosmetic products, including those used in barbershops and salons. The main things they're looking at:

1. **Product notification** — all cosmetic products sold in Turkey need to be registered in the national cosmetics database
2. **Labeling requirements** — proper ingredient lists, manufacturer info, batch numbers
3. **Safety documentation** — products need to have safety assessments on file

For barbershops specifically, the concern is usually about:
- Are you using products that are properly registered for the Turkish market?
- Do you have the product labels and can you show what's in them?

What products do you use in your shop? That would help me tell you if you're likely to have any issues.

mostly basic things... aftershave, cologne, the usual. but the main one is pomade. I use Erkek brand pomade... 15 years now. it is my signature, you could say. when a man sits in my chair and I finish with the Erkek pomade... the hold, the shine, the smell... my customers know that smell. it is part of the experience. 85 lira per tin now, used to be much less of course, but it is worth it

15 years with the same pomade — that's loyalty, and it sounds like it's become part of your shop's identity. When customers associate a specific smell and finish with your work, that's branding without even trying.

Erkek is a Turkish brand, which is good news for the regulation question. Domestic brands are generally already compliant with TİTCK requirements because they have to register to sell in the market. The bigger risks are with:
- Imported products without Turkish labels
- Products bought from unofficial distributors
- Homemade or unlabeled products

So for your Erkek pomade at 85 lira per tin, you should be fine. But here's what I'd recommend doing just to be safe:

1. **Keep a tin with the full label intact** — don't throw away or scrape off the label. Inspectors want to see the ingredient list, batch number, and manufacturer details.
2. **Ask your supplier for a certificate of compliance** — just a simple document showing the product is registered. Any reputable supplier should have this.
3. **Keep your purchase receipts** — shows you're buying through legitimate channels.

Do you buy directly from the Erkek distributor or through a general barber supply shop?

through my supplier Kemal... he has a warehouse in Üsküdar, supplies most of the barbershops on this side of the Bosphorus. a good man, reliable. he brings the pomade, the aftershave, razors, towels, everything. every two weeks like clockwork. I have been buying from him almost as long as I have been using Erkek

A reliable supplier you've had for over a decade — that's valuable. Kemal sounds like the kind of old-school distributor who keeps things running smoothly.

Here's what I'd suggest: next time Kemal comes for his delivery, just ask him two things:

1. "Do you have a compliance certificate for the Erkek pomade?" — He should be able to get this from the manufacturer. It's basically a document confirming the product is registered with TİTCK.
2. "Are there any products I should be worried about with the new regulations?" — He probably knows better than anyone which products in his catalog might have issues.

If Kemal has been supplying barbershops for that long, he's likely already dealt with this for other shops. He might even have the certificates ready.

For your own records, I'd suggest keeping a simple folder — physical or digital — with:
- Receipts from Kemal (last 6 months is usually enough)
- Product labels or photos of labels
- Any certificates he provides

That way if an inspector comes, you just pull out the folder. No stress. How often do you get inspections?

once a year... the hygiene inspection. they look at the towels, the sterilization, the razors... last year they complained about the towel storage. I had to buy new shelving, 450 lira. but products... they have not asked about products before. that is why I am a little nervous about this new regulation

That makes sense — if it's a new area they're checking, it's smart to get ahead of it rather than be surprised during the inspection.

The good news is you're already running a clean, legitimate operation. You use registered Turkish products from a known supplier. You're not cutting corners with cheap imports or unlabeled stuff. That puts you ahead of most shops already.

Here's a simple checklist to prepare:

**Before the next inspection:**
- [ ] Get compliance certificate from Kemal for your main products (Erkek pomade, aftershave, cologne)
- [ ] Take photos of all product labels — front and back — store on your phone
- [ ] Keep a small folder at the shop with receipts and certificates
- [ ] Make sure nothing in the shop is in an unlabeled container (sometimes barbers decant products into generic bottles — inspectors don't like that)

**Quick question:** Do you decant any products, or do you use everything straight from the original packaging?

the cologne I pour into the old glass bottles... the beautiful ones with the silver tops, you know. they have been in the shop since Hasan usta's time. part of the atmosphere. but they do not have labels on them...

Ah, those classic barbershop cologne bottles — they're iconic, I know exactly what you mean. They look beautiful and they're part of the traditional barbershop experience. But from a regulation standpoint, they could be a problem.

Inspectors want to know what's in every bottle. Unlabeled containers are a red flag because they can't verify the product is registered or safe.

**The solution is simple and preserves the atmosphere:**
- Make small, elegant labels for the bottles. Just the product name, brand, and "contents: [cologne name]" is enough.
- You can do this tastefully — a small tag tied around the neck of the bottle with a ribbon, or a small adhesive label on the bottom.
- Keep the original cologne bottles with full labels in storage as reference.

That way you keep Hasan usta's beautiful bottles AND satisfy the inspectors. The aesthetic doesn't have to suffer.

Or another approach: some barbers put a small framed card near the bottles listing what's in each one. Like a little menu card. That can actually look quite classy and professional.

Would either of those approaches work for you?

the tag with the ribbon... yes. Ayşe could make those, she has fabric and ribbon upstairs. she would make them beautiful, I am sure. better than anything I could do. and a small card... maybe both. one of the tourists might even think it is charming

That's a perfect solution — handmade tags by a seamstress, on antique cologne bottles, in a traditional barbershop. That's not just regulatory compliance, that's adding to the charm. Tourists would absolutely love it. It tells a story.

So here's your action plan:

1. **Ask Kemal for product certificates** next delivery (in about a week or so?)
2. **Have Ayşe make ribbon tags** for the cologne bottles — product name, brand on each tag
3. **Take photos of all product labels** and keep them on your phone
4. **Start a small folder** — receipts + certificates + a list of products you use
5. **Consider a small display card** near the cologne bottles listing the products

Total cost: basically zero, since Ayşe has the materials and everything else is just organizing what you already have.

You're going to be the most inspection-ready barbershop in Kadıköy. The inspector will walk in, see the antique bottles with handmade tags, the folder of certificates, and think "this man has his act together."

hah... that is the plan. I will tell Ayşe tonight. she likes a project, keeps her busy in the evenings. and I will call Kemal tomorrow about the certificates. 'hazırlıklı olan yenilmez'... the one who is prepared cannot be defeated. my friend, you have helped me again. her zamanki gibi... as always. iyi akşamlar

Another great proverb — and very fitting! You're doing exactly the right thing by getting ahead of this instead of waiting for the inspector to show up.

Give Ayşe the project tonight, call Kemal tomorrow, and you'll be fully prepared well before any inspection comes around. The combination of a well-run traditional shop and proper documentation is unbeatable.

İyi akşamlar, Mehmet! Give my regards to the Erkek pomade — 15 years of service deserves some respect. Take care!
